summary: Gruber analyzes Bloomberg's report about Apple pursuing paid search results in the App Store, arguing the better comparison isn't Google search but retail co-op placement like Amazon and grocery stores. He frames search terms as store aisles and paid promotions as endcaps, noting this model better explains the reported 100-person team (mostly salespeople). He thinks if done right it won't harm anything but won't fix existing App Store problems either. He also speculates that the leak to Bloomberg likely came from someone opposed to the program internally, not as a trial balloon from supporters.
key_points: The right analogy for App Store paid search isn't Google but retail co-op — pay-for-placement like grocery store endcaps and Amazon's co-op fees
The retail co-op model better explains the reported 100-person team, which would be mostly salespeople rather than engineers
Done right, paid placement is a neutral addition that won't fix the App Store's longstanding discovery problems
Done wrong, it could allow anti-competitive moves like buying ads on a competitor's app name
The leak likely came from an internal opponent who foresaw the negative reaction, not from someone trying to warm up the public
money_quotes: Perhaps comparisons to Google search are a red herring, and the right comparison is to Amazon, and retail co-op.
Think of search terms as being like the aisles in a grocery store, and the paid promotions are the endcaps.
If Apple does it right, I think it's just a new thing in the App Store that won't make anything worse — but won't address any of the longstanding problems, either.
If they do it wrong, they'll allow shitty things like buying your competitor's app's name.
Apple doesn't care about 'warming us up' to changes. They don't care.
I think it makes more sense as a leak from someone opposed to it, and who foresaw that it wouldn't go over well.
key_insight: App Store paid search is best understood not as Google-style advertising but as retail co-op placement — a neutral revenue addition that won't solve the App Store's real discovery problems.
